intent: Stop test_security_hardening.sh reporting healthy protection as broken. It checked systemd units that the guard-lib refactor renamed, and treated a deliberately retired unit as a fault.
scope: linux_configuration/tests/test_security_hardening.sh: guard unit checks and the shutdown-schedule guard check
Non-goal: any change to the guard units or system state. Nothing on the host was started, stopped, enabled or disabled.
changes: Tests 2, 3 and 5 checked hosts-guard.path, hosts-bind-mount.service and nsswitch-guard.path. The 2026-07-17 guard-lib refactor replaced these with templated units: guard-file@hosts.path, guard-bind-mount@hosts.service and guard-file@nsswitch.path. Each check now accepts either generation, so it passes on a migrated host and on one still running the legacy units.
Test 3 additionally accepts the read-only bind mount on /etc/hosts itself. The mount is the actual invariant; which unit established it is an implementation detail.

Test 10 (shutdown-schedule-guard.path) now distinguishes disabled from enabled-but-inactive. The guard was retired along with the poweroff flow — day-specific-shutdown now hands over to night-lockdown-enter.sh — so disabled is the intended state and is reported as a skip. An enabled unit that is not running is still a failure.

Fixed a shell bug in that check: `systemctl is-enabled` prints the state on stdout AND exits non-zero when the state is 'disabled', so `$(... || echo disabled)` produced two lines and never compared equal.

verification.evidence: 18 passed / 0 failed / 6 skipped, up from 15/4/5. The four reported failures were stale unit names plus one intentionally retired guard, not missing protection.

verification.command: systemctl is-active on the guard-lib units, plus lsattr and findmnt
verification.evidence: guard-file@hosts.path, guard-file@nsswitch.path, guard-bind-mount@hosts.service and etc-hosts.mount are all active; /etc/hosts is mounted ro,noatime; /etc/hosts and /etc/nsswitch.conf both carry the immutable flag. Protection was intact the whole time.

verification.command: negative check — fake the configured-host markers inside a container with no protection installed
verification.evidence: Reports 10 failures, confirming the suite still detects genuinely absent protection and has not been softened into always-green.

risks: Accepting either unit generation means a half-migrated host — new unit running, legacy unit failed — passes. That is the current state of this machine and is harmless, but it does mean the suite will not flag leftover failed legacy units. Those remain visible in `systemctl --failed`.

The legacy nsswitch-guard.path and resolved-guard.path are still enabled and sit in a failed state after boot: they and the new guard-file@ watchers both enforced the same files, so each retriggered itself into systemd's start limit. Cosmetic, since the new watchers do the work, but disabling the obsolete units would clean up `systemctl --failed`. Not done here because it changes system state.

Test 10 keys off `is-enabled`. If the shutdown guard is ever re-enabled without being started, that correctly fails; if it is re-introduced under a new name, this check needs updating the same way tests 2/3/5 just did.
rollback: git revert this commit: the suite returns to checking only the legacy unit names and reports 4 failures on this host despite protection being active.
Validate after rollback: bash test_security_hardening.sh prints FAIL for hosts-guard.path, hosts-bind-mount.service, nsswitch-guard.path and shutdown-schedule-guard.path.
